planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a French scab-resistant dessert apple.

directions: Plant in full sun; strong scab resistance makes it a low-spray favorite. Florina bears dark-red, sweet, mildly aromatic apples that keep well into winter. Harvest in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a Czech scab-resistant dessert apple.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained soil and prune for an open canopy. Rubinola offers sweet, aromatic, red-flushed apples with scab resistance and good flavor. Harvest in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a Dutch scab-resistant apple noted as low-allergen.

directions: Plant in full sun; this scab-resistant variety is often tolerated by people with apple allergies. Santana bears juicy, sweet-tart red-and-green apples for fresh eating. Harvest in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a narrow columnar tree for small spaces or pots.

directions: Plant in full sun in the ground or a large container, giving little sideways room since the tree grows as a single upright column. Fruit forms on short spurs along the trunk, so almost no pruning is needed. Harvest the crisp, greenish-yellow apples in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a compact columnar tree ideal for patios.

directions: Set the slim tree in full sun in a bed or large pot and keep it evenly watered. Its fruit clusters tightly along the upright stem, needing no framework pruning. Pick the sweet, red-flushed apples in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a hardy McIntosh-flavored columnar apple.

directions: Plant in full sun; the narrow habit fits tight spaces and containers. North Pole bears McIntosh-type red apples along its single upright trunk. Harvest in early to mid autumn when the fruit colors and softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a yellow-fruited columnar apple.

directions: Plant in full sun in a border or roomy container and stake if exposed to wind. Golden Sentinel produces sweet, golden-yellow apples densely along its upright stem. Harvest in early autumn once the skin turns clear gold.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; an extremely cold-hardy Russian cooking apple.

directions: Plant in full sun; this iron-hardy Russian variety survives the harshest winters and is widely used as rootstock. Antonovka bears tart, aromatic green-yellow apples best for cooking and juice. Harvest in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a long-keeping dual-purpose apple.

directions: Plant in full sun and provide a pollinator for good fruit set. A Jonathan and Delicious cross, Melrose is a large red apple that improves after a few weeks of storage. Harvest in mid to late autumn and cellar for winter use.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a late-blooming heirloom that dodges spring frosts.

directions: Plant in full sun; its very late bloom helps it escape late-frost damage. Ralls Janet, a parent of Fuji, bears mild, sweet, red-striped apples that store well. Harvest in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a traditional southern Italian apple.

directions: Plant in full sun in a warm, mild-winter climate. Annurca is traditionally harvested firm, then laid on straw beds to redden and ripen on the ground. Pick in autumn and finish the small, crisp red apples off the tree in the traditional way.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a late-keeping English dessert apple.

directions: Plant in full sun and prune to keep light in the canopy. A Cox's Orange Pippin descendant, it offers rich, aromatic flavor that intensifies after storage. Harvest in mid to late autumn and cellar into winter.

planting.note: Set out the grafted tree while dormant in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant this grafted tree with the graft union a few inches above the finished soil line and stake it for the first two seasons. This late Belgian pear ripens slowly, so pick it hard in October and store it cool for weeks until the flesh turns pink-tinged and perfumed. Thin heavy fruit clusters to one or two per spur to keep size up.

planting.note: Establish the bare-root tree in early spring in deep, well-drained loam.

directions: Dig a hole wide enough to spread the roots without bending and water deeply after backfilling. Provide a second flowering pear nearby for cross-pollination, since this old French variety sets poorly on its own. Harvest in early autumn while still firm and let the buttery, melting flesh finish ripening indoors.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring where blossoms will be safe from hard late frosts.

directions: This vigorous tree produces large, roundish fruit, so build a strong open framework with early-year pruning. Set the graft union clear of the soil and mulch the root zone without piling material against the trunk. Gather the pears in late summer and use them within a week or two, as they do not keep long.

planting.note: Site in full sun with shelter from strong wind to protect the crop.

directions: Plant the grafted tree at the same depth it grew in the nursery and firm the soil to remove air pockets. The long, russeted fruit colors attractively and hangs well into autumn. Pick when the pears part easily from the spur with a slight upward twist, then ripen a few days indoors.

planting.note: A hardy, reliable pear well suited to cooler northern gardens; plant in spring.

directions: This Belgian pear tolerates cold and cropping is dependable, making it a good choice for exposed sites. Water regularly through the first two summers until the roots reach out. Harvest the tapering fruit in early to mid autumn and eat soon after softening.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in fertile, moisture-retentive but free-draining soil.

directions: Grow this tender-fleshed autumn pear against a warm fence or in an open sunny spot for best flavor. Keep the young tree free of grass and weeds within a wide circle of the trunk. Pick when the fruit lifts cleanly and enjoy the melting flesh within a few days.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compatible pollinator improves this variety's set.

directions: This handsome russeted French pear is thought to be a Comice seedling and shares its rich flavor. Establish an open-center or central-leader shape and thin the fruit to improve size. Harvest in early autumn and store cool for a few weeks to develop full sweetness.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: A Bartlett-parent cross from New York, this pear carries a musky, sweet flavor and ripens after its Bartlett parent. Backfill the planting hole with native soil and water in well without adding raw fertilizer to the roots. Pick while firm and finish ripening off the tree.

planting.note: Set out in spring; this late variety needs a long season to finish.

directions: This Comice-by-Bartlett cross from Geneva, New York, keeps unusually well for a dessert pear. Give it a full-sun position and a second pear for pollination. Harvest in mid to late autumn and refrigerate; the fruit holds its quality for two to three months.

planting.note: Plant in spring with a pollination partner, as this variety is triploid.

directions: Because this English pear is triploid it needs two other compatible pears nearby to pollinate it and each other. Train a sturdy central leader and thin crowded fruit for best size. Pick in early autumn and eat while the juicy flesh is at its peak, as it does not store long.

planting.note: Best in milder zones with a long autumn to mature this late pear.

directions: This classic French winter pear is picked hard in late autumn and stored cool for eating from midwinter on. Plant in a sunny, sheltered site and provide a pollinator. Once stored fruit yields to gentle pressure at the neck, bring it to room temperature to finish softening.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a warm, sunny position.

directions: This large, early French pear ripens in late summer with soft, sweet flesh. Firm the soil around the roots at planting and keep the tree watered through dry spells. Harvest just before full ripeness because the fruit softens and passes quickly.

planting.note: This old triploid variety needs two pollinating partners; plant in spring.

directions: One of the oldest cultivated pears, this vigorous triploid tolerates cool, damp regions and can be trained as an espalier. Provide other flowering pears for pollination since it cannot fertilize itself. Pick the long, tapering fruit in late summer and eat within a few days of harvest.

planting.note: A tough Yorkshire pear for exposed, cool gardens; plant in spring.

directions: This hardy old English pear crops heavily and reliably even in unfavorable seasons. The fruit is small but sweet and juicy, ripening in early autumn. Plant in full sun, thin the abundant crop to improve size, and pick when the pears loosen on the spur.

planting.note: A long-lived cooking pear; plant in spring with room to grow large.

directions: This ancient culinary pear makes a large, long-lived tree and stores its hard fruit well into winter. The dense flesh is meant for cooking and slow baking rather than fresh eating. Give it space, a strong framework, and simply pick the fruit late and store it cool until needed.

planting.note: This triploid cooking pear needs two pollinators nearby; plant in spring.

directions: Catillac is a large, triploid cooking pear whose hard flesh turns pink and sweet when stewed for a long time. Because it is triploid, grow two other flowering pears nearby to ensure pollination. Harvest the big fruit in late autumn and store cool for use through winter.

planting.note: Reliable in cool districts; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Also known as Curé, this long, tapering pear is grown mainly for cooking and crops heavily. It tolerates cooler climates and is a dependable garden tree. Pick the fruit in late autumn while still firm and stew or bake it, or store cool for later use.

planting.note: A warm-climate Italian pear; plant in spring in a sunny site.

directions: This early Italian pear bears small, sweet fruit with pale, delicate flesh and thrives in warm Mediterranean-type climates. Plant in full sun and water steadily through the fruiting period. Harvest in mid to late summer and eat promptly, as the soft fruit does not keep.

planting.note: A fire-blight-resistant U.S. release; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Released from the U.S. breeding program, this golden russeted pear offers good fire blight resistance and aromatic, sweet flesh. Plant in full sun with a compatible pollinator and keep the base weed-free. Pick in early autumn while firm and ripen indoors for a few days before eating.

planting.note: Fire-blight-tolerant and Anjou-like; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: This U.S.-bred pear resembles D'Anjou in shape and has good resistance to fire blight. Set the tree at nursery depth, water it in, and provide another flowering pear for cross-pollination. Harvest in early to mid autumn and store cool briefly to develop the mild, sweet flavor.

planting.note: An early, blight-resistant pear; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Sunrise is an early-ripening U.S. pear with attractive blushed skin and good fire blight resistance. Plant in a sunny, open position with a second pear for pollination. Pick in late summer while still firm and let the smooth, sweet flesh finish softening off the tree.

planting.note: Blight-tolerant and very sweet; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: This U.S.-bred pear lives up to its name with rich, honeyed sweetness and shows useful fire blight resistance. Give it full sun, another flowering pear for pollination, and steady moisture while establishing. Harvest in early autumn and ripen a few days indoors before eating.

planting.note: A very early Bulgarian pear with blight tolerance; plant in spring.

directions: This Bulgarian variety is among the earliest pears to ripen and shows good resistance to fire blight. Plant in full sun with a compatible pollinator for reliable crops. Pick the blushed fruit in mid to late summer and eat soon, since early pears soften quickly.

planting.note: Also sold as Starking Delicious; blight-resistant, plant in spring.

directions: Sold in some regions as Starking Delicious, this pear has good fire blight resistance and firm, white, sweet flesh. Plant in full sun with a second pear for pollination and keep the young tree watered. Harvest in early autumn while firm and ripen indoors before eating.

planting.note: An early, richly flavored pear; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: This Marguerite Marillat by Bartlett cross ripens early with large, aromatic, melting fruit. Plant in full sun, provide a compatible pear for pollination, and thin the crop for size. Pick in late summer to early autumn while firm and finish ripening off the tree.

planting.note: One of the earliest French pears; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This early French pear delivers juicy, sweet fruit at the very start of the pear season. Plant in full sun with a pollinating partner and water regularly through the summer. Harvest in midsummer just before full ripeness and eat within a few days.

planting.note: An early summer French pear; plant in spring in a sunny spot.

directions: This old French summer pear bears small to medium fruit with tender, melting, sweet flesh. The tree is modest in vigor, so avoid crowding it and keep it well fed. Harvest in midsummer while just firm and use quickly, as it ripens and softens fast.

planting.note: A late-keeping French pear; plant in spring in a warm site.

directions: This flattened, russeted French pear stores well and is eaten in late winter. Give it a warm, sunny position and a pollinator to set a good crop. Pick in late autumn while hard and hold in cool storage until the flesh becomes rich and buttery.

planting.note: A large Bartlett-related pear; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: This large French pear resembles a big, early Bartlett with a bright red blush on the sunny side. Plant in full sun with a compatible pollinator and thin heavy crops to keep the fruit large. Harvest in late summer while firm and ripen indoors before eating fresh.

planting.note: A late winter-keeping pear; plant in spring in a sheltered sunny site.

directions: This late French pear is picked hard and stored to ripen through the winter months. Plant it in a warm, sheltered position with a pollinator to ensure a full crop. Gather the fruit in late autumn and keep it cool until the flesh softens and sweetens.

planting.note: A showy large-fruited pear; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: This French pear produces large, handsome fruit with a bright red cheek and is often grown as a decorative espalier. It sets poorly with its own pollen, so plant a compatible partner nearby. Harvest in early autumn while firm and ripen off the tree for best texture.

planting.note: Very cold hardy; plant in spring in northern gardens.

directions: Bred in Minnesota, this exceptionally cold-hardy pear bears small, spicy, tart fruit best for canning and cooking. Plant in full sun with another hardy pear for pollination. Harvest in early autumn once the fruit colors up and use it for preserves or spiced pears.

planting.note: A hardy Minnesota pear; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: This Minnesota introduction is hardy through cold winters and produces medium to large, mild, juicy fruit for fresh eating. Plant in full sun with another flowering pear for pollination. Pick in late summer to early autumn while firm and ripen indoors before eating.

planting.note: A hardy Iowa pear; plant in spring with a compatible pollinator.

directions: Introduced from Iowa, this hardy pear yields large, juicy, sweet fruit and pairs well as a pollinator for other cold-climate pears. Plant in full sun and keep the root zone mulched and watered while young. Harvest in early autumn while firm and finish ripening off the tree.

planting.note: Extremely hardy; plant in spring in far-northern gardens.

directions: This Canadian-bred pear is among the hardiest available and survives severe prairie winters. It makes a compact tree with small, sweet, greenish fruit good for fresh use and cooking. Plant in full sun with another hardy pear for pollination and pick in late summer.

planting.note: A hardy South Dakota Asian-type pear; plant in spring.

directions: This South Dakota introduction gives crisp, sweet, apple-shaped Asian-style fruit on a cold-hardy tree. Plant in full sun with another compatible pear for pollination. Harvest when the fruit is fully colored and eat it fresh and crisp straight from the tree.

planting.note: A hardy pear; plant in spring with another hardy variety.

directions: This hardy pear tolerates cold northern climates and bears sweet, juicy fruit for fresh eating. Plant in full sun with a compatible hardy pear nearby for pollination. Harvest in late summer to early autumn while firm and ripen briefly before eating.

planting.note: Plant in spring with another Asian pear for pollination.

directions: This green-skinned Japanese nashi bears flat, round fruit with crisp, juicy, sweet flesh. Plant in full sun with a second Asian pear for cross-pollination and thin the fruit heavily to size it up. Pick when fully colored and eat crisp; Asian pears ripen on the tree.

planting.note: A large, late Asian pear; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: Atago produces very large, russeted, round fruit that ripens late in the season with sweet, crisp flesh. Plant in full sun with another Asian pear for pollination and thin aggressively to reach full size. Harvest in mid to late autumn once the fruit turns brown-gold.

planting.note: An early Asian pear; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: This early Japanese nashi ripens ahead of most Asian pears with sweet, russeted, juicy fruit. Plant in full sun with a compatible pollinator and thin developing fruit for size. Harvest in mid to late summer once the skin browns and the fruit tastes sweet.

planting.note: A very large Asian pear; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: Mishirasu is prized for its enormous russeted fruit that can weigh over a pound with crisp, sweet, juicy flesh. Plant in full sun with another Asian pear for pollination and thin hard to concentrate size. Harvest in autumn once the fruit is fully colored and firm-crisp.

planting.note: A late, storing Asian pear; plant in spring with a pollinator.

directions: This late-season Korean pear is one of the better keepers among Asian pears, with firm, sweet, mildly acidic flesh. Plant in full sun with a compatible pollinator and thin the crop for size. Pick in mid to late autumn and store cool, where quality improves over several weeks.

planting.note: Plant in spring with another Asian pear for pollination.

directions: This Japanese nashi produces round, russeted fruit with sweet, crisp, juicy flesh in the mid to late season. Plant in full sun with a compatible pollinator and thin the young fruit for size and quality. Harvest when fully colored and eat crisp, as the fruit ripens on the tree.

planting.note: A traditional perry pear that makes a large, long-lived tree; give it room.

directions: This classic English perry pear grows into a very large, long-lived standard tree and takes years to crop but then bears heavily. The small, red-flushed fruit is milled and pressed for perry rather than eaten fresh. Plant in full sun with plenty of space and simply gather the fruit as it drops in autumn.

planting.note: A vintage perry pear for a large standard tree; give it space.

directions: A renowned English perry variety, Yellow Huffcap yields fruit prized for high-quality perry. The tree becomes large and productive but is slow to reach full bearing. Plant in full sun with ample room, then collect the fruit at the shake stage in autumn for milling and pressing.

subcategory: stone fruit, low-chill yellow freestone peach

planting.note: Suited to mild-winter regions; needs only a few hundred chill hours to fruit.

directions: This low-chill freestone thrives where winters are too warm for northern peaches, so plant it in a sunny, well-drained spot in the Gulf and Southwest. Set the graft union above grade, prune to an open vase, and thin the fruit in spring. The large yellow peaches ripen early and are ready when their color deepens and the flesh softens.

planting.note: A hardy Canadian selection; plant in early spring in colder peach country.

directions: This hardy self-fertile freestone was bred for reliable cropping in cooler regions, so give it your sunniest, best-drained site. Set the union above grade and prune annually to keep the canopy open to light. Thin in early summer and pick the sweet, red-skinned fruit in mid-summer when it parts easily from the stem.

subcategory: stone fruit, early yellow semi-freestone peach

planting.note: An early-ripening Redhaven sport; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: A hardy early sport of Redhaven, this self-fertile peach delivers a crop weeks ahead of the main season. Plant it in full sun with the graft clear of the soil and thin the set to size the fruit. The garnet-blushed, semi-freestone flesh ripens in early to mid-summer and is best used soon after picking.

subcategory: stone fruit, large yellow freestone peach

planting.note: Plant bare-root in early spring in a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: This large, nearly fuzzless self-fertile freestone is a favorite for both fresh eating and freezing. Plant in full sun with the graft above the soil and give steady summer water to size the big fruit. Thin the crop in June and pick in late summer when the firm yellow flesh yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: An Elberta-type freestone; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: A refined selection in the Elberta family, this self-fertile freestone offers large, richly flavored fruit for canning and fresh use. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and keep the union above grade. Prune to an open center, thin in early summer, and harvest in late summer when the golden fruit softens and pulls free of the stem.

subcategory: stone fruit, low-chill yellow freestone peach

planting.note: A moderate-chill Southern peach for the Gulf and lower South.

directions: Bred for the mild-winter South, this self-fertile freestone fruits well where northern peaches never get enough chilling. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and set the graft above grade. Thin the fruit in spring; the sweet yellow flesh ripens by early to mid-summer and separates freely from the pit when ripe.

subcategory: stone fruit, early yellow clingstone peach

planting.note: An early-season clingstone; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: One of the earliest yellow peaches to ripen, this self-fertile clingstone rewards gardeners who want fruit ahead of the main flush. Plant in full sun with the union above grade and thin the crop so the small early fruit can size up. Pick in early summer when the bright red-over-yellow skin softens; the flesh clings to the pit.

planting.note: An early-ripening yellow peach; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This early self-fertile peach carries firm, low-fuzz fruit with a bright red blush over gold. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and keep the graft union clear of the soil line. Thin heavily in early summer to size the fruit, and harvest in mid-summer when the flesh softens and loosens from the pit.

planting.note: A dependable mid-season freestone; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This hardy self-fertile freestone is a consistent producer valued for canning and fresh eating alike. Plant in full sun with good drainage and set the union above grade. Thin the crop in early summer, prune to an open vase each winter, and harvest in mid-summer when the fruit colors and parts freely from the stone.

planting.note: An early to mid-season peach; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This self-fertile peach carries a heavy set of firm, attractive fruit and needs thinning to reach full size. Plant in full sun with the union above grade and mulch the root zone. Prune to an open center in winter and pick the red-blushed fruit in mid-summer when the ground color turns gold and the flesh yields.

planting.note: An aromatic white-flesh heirloom; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This old white freestone is beloved for its soft, juicy, richly perfumed flesh, though it bruises easily and does not ship. Plant in full sun with good drainage and keep the union above grade. Thin the crop in early summer and harvest in mid-summer, handling the tender fruit gently as it softens on the tree.

planting.note: A sweet white peach; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This self-fertile white peach carries very sweet, low-acid flesh under a deep red skin and rates highly in taste trials. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and set the union above grade. Thin in early summer to size the fruit and pick in mid-summer once the fruit softens and its aroma fills the air.

subcategory: stone fruit, cold-hardy white freestone peach

planting.note: A hardy white peach for the upper Midwest; plant in early spring.

directions: This hardy old white freestone was selected for the Midwest and tolerates colder winters than most white peaches. Plant in full sun with the best drainage and air movement you can offer, keeping the union above grade. Thin the crop in early summer and harvest the creamy, blush-cheeked fruit in late summer when it softens.

planting.note: An aromatic white peach; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This self-fertile white freestone is famed for melting, honey-sweet, intensely fragrant flesh. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and keep the graft union clear of the soil. Thin the fruit in early summer and pick in mid-summer when the pale fruit yields to a light touch and perfumes the orchard.

subcategory: stone fruit, flat white-fleshed donut peach

planting.note: A flat donut peach; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: A flat peach in the doughnut class, this self-fertile tree produces sweet white-fleshed fruit with a pleasing almond note. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and keep the union above grade. Thin the crop in early summer so the saucer-shaped fruit sizes up, and pick in mid-summer when the fruit softens and colors.

subcategory: stone fruit, low-chill flat donut peach

planting.note: A low-chill flat peach suited to mild-winter gardens.

directions: This low-chill flat peach lets warm-winter gardeners grow the popular doughnut type where cold hours are scarce. Plant in full sun with good drainage and keep the graft above grade. Thin the set in spring and harvest in early to mid-summer when the saucer-shaped, white-fleshed fruit softens and its skin blushes.

planting.note: A late-ripening heirloom clingstone; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This old red-fleshed clingstone is prized for preserves, its blood-red flesh streaked toward the pit. Plant in full sun with good drainage, keep the union above grade, and prune to an open center. Thin in early summer and harvest very late in the season; the tart-sweet flesh clings firmly to the stone and makes striking pickles and jams.

subcategory: stone fruit, yellow clingstone canning peach

planting.note: A firm canning clingstone; plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: A classic yellow canning clingstone, this self-fertile peach holds firm, deep-colored flesh that keeps its shape in the jar. Plant in full sun with good drainage and keep the union above grade. Thin the crop in early summer and harvest late in the season when the firm fruit reaches full yellow color; the flesh stays attached to the pit.

subcategory: stone fruit, low-chill white freestone nectarine

planting.note: A very low-chill white nectarine for mild coastal gardens.

directions: This very low-chill self-fertile nectarine is a top pick for mild coastal and Southern gardens, with sweet white flesh under a dark red skin. Plant in full sun with good drainage and air flow and keep the union above grade. Thin the crop in spring and harvest early in the season when the fruit softens and its aroma builds.

planting.note: A genetic dwarf for patios and containers; plant in early spring.

directions: This genetic-dwarf nectarine stays small enough for a large pot or a small yard, yet bears full-size yellow fruit. Plant in full sun in a container or well-drained bed and water regularly, as dwarfs in pots dry out fast. It benefits from a nearby pollinator such as a miniature peach; thin the fruit and harvest in mid-summer when it softens.

subcategory: stone fruit, low-chill yellow clingstone nectarine

planting.note: A low-chill Florida nectarine for the Deep South; plant in early spring.

directions: This low-chill self-fertile nectarine was developed for the Deep South and fruits where winters barely chill. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and air movement and keep the union above grade. Thin the crop in spring and harvest early in the season when the bright red fruit softens; the yellow flesh clings to the pit.

planting.note: Set dormant trees out in early spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Give this old dessert gage a warm wall or sheltered position, as it fruits best in a long, mild summer. Plant at the nursery soil mark and mulch to conserve moisture around the shallow roots. Harvest the small round green-gold plums in late summer when they turn honey-sweet and translucent.

scientific_name: Prunus domestica subsp. syriaca 'Mirabelle de Nancy'

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: Firm this self-fertile French mirabelle into well-drained ground and water through its first dry spells. The tree carries heavy clusters of small golden cherry-plums, so thinning improves size and prevents limb breakage. Gather the sweet amber fruit in late summer for fresh eating, tarts, or preserves once they drop freely into the hand.

scientific_name: Prunus domestica subsp. syriaca 'Mirabelle de Metz'

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root trees in early spring.

directions: Set this hardy self-fertile mirabelle in a sunny, freely draining site and keep the root zone weed-free. It produces masses of tiny freckled golden plums that are smaller and slightly earlier than the Nancy type. Pick in late summer when the fruit is fully colored and softens for eau-de-vie, jam, or fresh snacking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this hardy, self-fertile cooking plum in full sun; it tolerates poorer sites than most dessert varieties. The reliable tree yields oval bright-yellow fruit valued for jam and stewing. Harvest in late summer when the skins turn clear yellow and the flesh softens, cooking down to a tart, golden puree.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: This hardy, self-fertile American gage crops reliably even in cooler summers, making it easier than most true gages. Plant in full sun and stake young trees until anchored. Pick the round greenish-yellow fruit in late summer once it turns translucent and develops its sweet, gage-like richness.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a sheltered site.

directions: Site this exceptional dessert gage against a warm wall, as it fruits best with plenty of summer heat. The self-fertile tree bears yellow plums so ripe they become almost translucent, showing the stone through the flesh. Harvest in late summer when fully soft and richly aromatic, eating soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a warm position.

directions: Give this late-ripening dessert gage a warm, sheltered spot and full sun to develop its exceptional sweetness. Self-fertile and richly flavored, it produces large golden plums that turn translucent when fully ripe. Harvest in early autumn when the fruit is soft, deeply amber, and drips with juice.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: This hardy, self-fertile damson tolerates exposed and cold sites, so plant it where dessert plums would struggle. The tree carries large blue-black fruit that is bigger than most damsons and good for jam, gin, and cooking. Harvest in early autumn when the fruit turns fully black with a heavy bloom and the flesh softens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this tough, heavy-cropping damson in full sun, ideally near a pollinizer to boost its variable set. The compact tree produces abundant small blue-black fruit traditionally grown for preserves. Pick in early autumn once the damsons color fully and cook down to a rich, tart, deep-purple pulp.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Set this self-fertile heritage damson in a sunny, freely draining site; it is slow to establish but long-lived and reliable. The small oval blue-black fruit has the intense, spicy flavor prized for damson cheese and preserves. Harvest in autumn when fully colored, tasting sharply astringent raw but superb after cooking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this self-fertile damson in full sun and give it space, as it forms a rounded, productive tree. The large, deep reddish-purple fruit is richly flavored and among the finest damsons for culinary use. Harvest in early autumn when the fruit fully colors and the flesh yields, then use promptly for jam and bottling.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this early, heavy-cropping culinary plum in full sun with a pollination partner nearby. It ripens well ahead of most European plums, carrying small blue fruit with a bloom that is excellent for cooking. Thin the crowded clusters and harvest in midsummer as the plums soften and darken.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this vigorous, late-ripening Central European plum in full sun; it is self-fertile and dependable. The large reddish-purple fruit has sweet, juicy flesh good for dessert and cooking alike. Harvest in autumn when the deep color and dusty bloom develop and the plums soften slightly on the branch.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this classic French drying prune in full sun with excellent drainage and warm summers for the highest sugar. Self-fertile and productive, it yields small reddish-purple freestone fruit that is the traditional source of dried prunes. Harvest in late summer to early fall when the fruit is fully sweet, then dry or eat fresh.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: This early Burbank prune is self-fertile and ripens weeks ahead of Agen, so plant it in full sun for a longer drying window. The dark purple freestone fruit is exceptionally high in sugar and dries easily without pitting. Harvest in late summer when fully colored and soft, using fresh or for homemade dried prunes.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this early Swedish variety in full sun; it is self-fertile and reliably productive even in cooler climates. Herman is one of the earliest blue plums, carrying medium blue-black fruit good fresh or cooked. Harvest in mid to late summer when the plums color fully and soften on the tree.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this productive, late-ripening prune-type plum in full sun; it is partially self-fertile but crops better with a pollinizer. The large blue-black freestone fruit is good fresh, canned, or dried. Harvest in early autumn once the deep color and dusty bloom develop and the flesh sweetens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this vigorous Serbian variety in full sun; it is self-fertile and an early, heavy cropper. The oval blue fruit with a violet bloom has sweet, freestone flesh suited to dessert and processing. Harvest in late summer as the plums color fully and soften, thinning early to prevent overbearing.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: This traditional German zwetsche is self-fertile and hardy, so plant it in full sun for its famously long-lived crops. The small, elongated dark-blue freestone fruit is the classic choice for baking, drying, and plum tarts. Harvest in early autumn when the plums fully color and the firm flesh sweetens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this early Swedish dessert plum in full sun; it sets more reliably with a nearby pollinizer. Jubileum bears very large dark reddish-blue fruit with sweet, juicy flesh resembling Victoria. Harvest in late summer once the plums color fully and soften slightly, eating soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this hardy, self-fertile cooking plum in full sun; it tolerates less favorable sites than dessert types. The oval purple fruit with a blue bloom cooks down well for jam and pies. Harvest in late summer once the skins darken and the flesh softens, using promptly for preserving.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this old large-fruited culinary plum in full sun with a pollination partner for good set. The big oval yellow fruit is firm and tart, long favored for canning and preserving. Harvest in late summer when the skins turn clear golden-yellow and the flesh yields slightly.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; well suited to mild-winter regions.

directions: Plant this low-chill blood plum in full sun with a Japanese-plum pollinizer for reliable crops. Mariposa offers sweet, nearly clingless red flesh with a mild, low-acid flavor. Harvest in midsummer when the mottled red skin colors fully and the fruit softens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a mild-winter area.

directions: Plant this very late Japanese plum in full sun with a pollinizer to extend the harvest season into autumn. Angeleno bears firm purple-black fruit with sweet amber flesh that stores exceptionally well. Harvest in early fall when the deep color and heavy bloom develop and the flesh firms up sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a hardy hybrid for cold regions.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy Minnesota hybrid in full sun with a pollinizer such as Toka, as it is not self-fertile. Superior produces large red-skinned fruit with firm yellow flesh, ripening in late summer. Harvest when the plums color fully and yield slightly, thinning the crop earlier to improve fruit size.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this classic light-fleshed variety in full sun with a compatible pollinizer such as Bing or Van. The yellow fruit blushed with red is the traditional choice for brining and preserves as well as fresh eating. Harvest in early to midsummer when the blush deepens and the flesh turns tender.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Set the tree in a sunny, well-drained site and provide a compatible pollinizer such as Bing or Rainier. Tieton is an early season variety producing very large, firm dark cherries. Harvest in early summer as soon as the fruit colors up fully.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in fertile soil and pair with a pollinizer for reliable set. This Czech variety bears large, firm, dark heart-shaped fruit on long stems. Harvest in mid to late summer when the cherries reach deep mahogany color.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this English variety in a sunny, sheltered site with a pollinizer nearby. Merton Glory produces very large, pale yellow fruit flushed with red early in the season. Harvest as the blush develops and the flesh softens in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this Canadian variety in full sun with good air movement to reduce disease. Vega bears large, firm cream-colored fruit and also serves as a useful pollinizer. Harvest the pale cherries in midsummer once they reach full size and a faint blush.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep loam and provide a pollinizer such as Kordia or Regina. Summit produces very large, firm heart-shaped fruit with rich flavor. Harvest the dark-red cherries in midsummer as they reach full color.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Set the tree in a sunny, well-drained spot with a pollinizer such as Bing or Rainier. Selah is a Washington-bred variety producing large, firm dark cherries with good crack resistance. Harvest in midsummer once the fruit reaches deep color and sweetness.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this old Canadian variety in full sun with a compatible pollinizer. Windsor is a hardy, dependable tree bearing firm dark-red fruit suited to both fresh eating and cooking. Harvest in mid to late summer once the cherries color fully.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil with a pollinizer for a full crop. This vigorous old variety bears large, firm, deep-mahogany fruit with rich flavor. Harvest in midsummer when the cherries darken to nearly black.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this New York-bred variety in full sun with a compatible pollinizer nearby. Ulster produces firm, sweet dark-red fruit with good crack resistance. Harvest in midsummer once the cherries reach deep, glossy color.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this classic variety in full sun with a pollinizer such as Van or Rainier. Lambert bears large, firm, dark heart-shaped fruit with rich, sweet flavor late in the season. Harvest in mid to late summer once the cherries turn deep red to black.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this early French variety in full sun with a compatible pollinizer. Burlat is one of the first sweet cherries to ripen, bearing large, dark-red heart-shaped fruit. Harvest in early summer as soon as the cherries color fully.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this New York-bred variety in full sun in well-drained soil. Black Gold is self-fertile and blooms late, which helps it dodge spring frosts. Harvest the firm, dark-red to black fruit in midsummer once fully colored.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this New York-bred variety in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil. White Gold is self-fertile, late-blooming, and produces yellow fruit blushed with red that resists cracking. Harvest in midsummer once the blush develops and the flesh softens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this old American variety in full sun with a compatible pollinizer nearby. Governor Wood bears tender, light-colored fruit blushed with red, best eaten straight from the tree. Harvest in early to midsummer as the fruit softens and colors up.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy variety in full sun with a compatible pollinizer. Kristin is prized in colder regions for its winter hardiness and firm, dark-red fruit. Harvest in midsummer once the cherries reach full color and sweetness.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this Canadian variety in full sun with the graft above the soil line. Santina is self-fertile, early ripening, and produces firm dark fruit that resists cracking. Harvest in early summer as soon as the cherries turn glossy black.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this variety in full sun with excellent drainage and a compatible pollinizer. Sonata produces large, firm dark-red fruit with sweet, rich flavor. Harvest in mid to late summer once the cherries reach deep color.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this exceptionally cold-hardy Canadian variety in full sun; it is self-fertile and thrives in northern gardens. Evans forms a tidy tree that suckers freely and can be grown as a shrub. Harvest the bright-red, tart fruit in midsummer for juice, pies, and jam.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this Minnesota-bred hybrid in full sun in well-drained soil; it is self-fertile and cold hardy. Mesabi has a milder, sweeter flavor than a typical pie cherry, good for fresh eating and cooking. Harvest the dark-red fruit in midsummer once fully colored.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this Scandinavian variety in full sun in well-drained soil; it is self-fertile. Jubileum bears large, dark-red fruit that is sweet enough to enjoy fresh yet still good for cooking. Harvest in early to midsummer once the cherries darken fully.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this New York-bred variety in full sun; it is self-fertile and blooms late to escape spring frosts. Surefire bears bright-red fruit that holds well on the tree without cracking. Harvest in mid to late summer for pies, juice, and preserves.

subcategory: stone fruit, dwarf sour cherry (Romance series)

planting.note: Plant the shrub in early spring after the ground thaws.

directions: Plant this University of Saskatchewan bush cherry in full sun; it is self-fertile and extremely cold hardy. The compact shrub form makes harvesting and bird netting easy. Pick the dark-red, sweet-tart fruit in mid to late summer once fully colored for fresh eating or cooking.

subcategory: stone fruit, dwarf sour cherry (Romance series)

planting.note: Plant the shrub in early spring after the ground thaws.

directions: Plant this hardy Saskatchewan bush cherry in full sun in well-drained soil; it is self-fertile. Romeo bears very dark, richly flavored fruit on a compact multi-stemmed shrub. Harvest late in the season once the cherries turn nearly black and sweeten.

subcategory: stone fruit, dwarf sour cherry (Romance series)

planting.note: Plant the shrub in early spring after the ground thaws.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy Saskatchewan bush cherry in full sun; it is self-fertile and forms a tidy shrub. Valentine produces bright-red, tart fruit well suited to pies, juice, and preserves. Harvest in mid to late summer once the cherries reach full red color.

planting.note: Prized for very large, showy fruit; plant in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant the tree at nursery depth and support it until the framework is established. Robada produces some of the largest apricots available, with a bright orange skin heavily flushed red and a rich, sweet flavor. Thin developing fruit to reach full size and harvest when the flesh softens slightly at the stem.

planting.note: Plant near a Japanese plum or pluot for cross-pollination.

directions: Set the tree in full sun and provide a compatible plum or pluot pollinizer nearby. Flavorella carries golden-yellow fruit with a translucent, juicy flesh and a tangy apricot-like flavor that ripens in early summer. Thin the heavy set and harvest when the fruit gives slightly and colors fully gold.

planting.note: Plant with a Japanese plum or another pluot for pollination.

directions: Plant in full sun with a suitable pollinizer and stake the young tree its first year. Splash produces small, intensely sweet reddish-orange fruit with orange flesh that many consider among the best-tasting pluots. Thin developing fruit and pick when the pluots soften and reach full color.

planting.note: Needs a compatible plum or pluot pollinizer for good fruit set.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained site and keep a pollinizer within range. Geo Pride ripens red-skinned fruit with firm orange flesh that balances sweetness with a pleasant tartness near the skin. Thin in spring for size and harvest when the fruit colors deeply and softens at the tip.

planting.note: One of the earliest pluots; plant with a plum or pluot pollinizer.

directions: Set the tree in full sun with a companion pollinizer nearby. Flavorosa is among the first pluots to ripen, bearing flat, dark-purple fruit with sweet, juicy red flesh. Thin the crop early and pick when the skin turns deep purple and the fruit yields to light pressure.

planting.note: An apricot-dominant hybrid; an apricot nearby improves the set.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the soil evenly moist as fruit develops. Honey Rich leans toward its apricot parent, ripening early with sweet, firm orange flesh and a smooth skin. Thin the fruit for size and harvest when the apricot-like fruit softens and colors fully.

planting.note: One of the earliest apriums; pair with an apricot for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in full sun with an apricot pollinizer and mulch to steady soil moisture. Tasty Rich ripens very early, offering sweet, aromatic fruit with the flavor of a rich apricot and a hint of plum. Thin the young fruit and pick when it colors fully and softens slightly.

planting.note: A cherry-plum hybrid; plant near a Japanese plum or pluot for pollination.

directions: Plant in full sun with a compatible plum or pluot pollinizer nearby. This cherry-plum cross bears clusters of small, deep-red fruit that eat like an extra-sweet cherry with plum undertones. Thin lightly if the set is heavy and harvest when the fruit turns fully red and tastes sweet.

planting.note: A cherry crossed with a plum; plant with a Japanese plum for pollination.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot with a Japanese plum nearby to set fruit. Nadia produces plum-sized dark fruit with a flavor that blends sweet cherry and plum, ripening in early summer. Thin heavy sets and pick when the skin turns near-black and the flesh softens.

planting.note: A self-fruitful European prune plum widely grown in cooler regions.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and prune to a central leader while young. Stanley is a dependable, self-fruitful prune plum bearing dark blue freestone fruit that is excellent fresh, canned, or dried. Harvest in late summer when the fruit turns deep purple with a dusty bloom and softens.

planting.note: Set out dormant vines in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant dormant, one-year-old vines with the graft or crown just at soil level and train a single trunk up a sturdy two-wire trellis. Prune hard each dormant season to a few renewal canes, since this vigorous University of Minnesota hybrid fruits heavily. Harvest the deep blue-black clusters in early fall when sugars climb and the tart cherry aroma develops for red wine or juice.

planting.note: Plant in early spring after the soil has thawed and drains freely.

directions: Space vines about six to eight feet apart on a high trellis in a sunny, well-drained site. This disease-resistant red hybrid tends to bud early, so choose a spot with good air movement to reduce spring frost risk. Pick the small, dark clusters in late summer to early fall when they reach high sugar for a spicy, Pinot-like wine.

planting.note: Set dormant vines in spring once frost has left the ground.

directions: Train this vigorous white hybrid on a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal spurs each winter. Its trailing habit responds well to high-cordon training, and thinning shoots improves airflow. Harvest the amber-gold clusters in fall when the fruit turns aromatic with apricot and honey notes for aromatic white and dessert wines.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Set vines six to eight feet apart and train to a two- or three-wire trellis, keeping the trunk single and straight. This Swenson red hybrid is quite winter hardy and moderately disease resistant, needing only routine dormant pruning. Harvest the blue-black clusters in early fall for a low-tannin red wine or fresh juice.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring after soils warm slightly.

directions: Train this vigorous, disease-resistant white hybrid on a high-cordon trellis in full sun. It sets large crops, so thin clusters to keep fruit quality high and prune to renewal canes each dormant season. Harvest the golden berries in late summer when they develop a pineapple-like aroma for white wine or fresh eating.

planting.note: Set out vines in early spring once the soil is workable.

directions: Plant this hardy Swenson selection in full sun and train the vigorous trailing shoots to a high trellis. It bears reliable crops of pale green to gold clusters and needs standard dormant pruning to a few fruiting canes. Pick slightly early for fresh eating or juice, as fully ripe fruit takes on a strong labrusca character.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this vine tolerates very cold sites.

directions: One of the hardiest grapes available, Valiant thrives in northern gardens where most vines fail. Train it to a sturdy trellis and prune hard each winter to control its vigor. Harvest the small blue Concord-like clusters in late summer for juice, jelly, and cold-climate winemaking.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring.

directions: Grow this hardy dual-purpose vine on a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes in late winter. The medium-large red berries hang in loose clusters and benefit from cluster thinning for size. Pick when the fruit turns fully red and sweet in late summer for fresh eating or homemade wine.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a warm, well-drained site.

directions: Train this Tom Plocher red hybrid to a high-cordon trellis in full sun and prune to short spurs. It ripens with soft tannins and low acidity, making it a favorite for structured cold-climate reds. Harvest the small dark clusters in early to mid fall once sugars are high.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring after the ground thaws.

directions: This University of Minnesota white hybrid combines strong winter hardiness with naturally low acidity. Train to a high trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes each dormant season. Harvest the greenish-gold clusters in fall when they show pear and melon aromas for a crisp dry white.

planting.note: Plant in spring once soils are workable.

directions: This productive Cornell white hybrid is disease resistant and easy to grow on a standard trellis. Prune to renewal canes and thin excess shoots to keep the heavy crop in balance. Harvest the pale clusters in early fall for a fruity, off-dry white wine.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a site with good air drainage.

directions: Grow this French hybrid on a trellis in full sun and stay ahead of downy and powdery mildew with an open canopy. Prune to a moderate number of fruiting canes each winter. Harvest the medium blue-black clusters in mid fall for a soft, fruity red wine.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: This vigorous, productive French hybrid ripens early and adapts well to cool regions. Train to a two-wire trellis and thin clusters to prevent overcropping, which can weaken the vine. Harvest the dark blue berries in early to mid fall for red and rose wines.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a warm site.

directions: A close relative of Marechal Foch, this early-ripening French hybrid produces small dark clusters on a hardy, disease-tolerant vine. Train to a trellis and prune to renewal canes each dormant season. Harvest in early fall for a deeply colored, fruity red wine.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: This early, cold-tolerant French hybrid is one of the oldest widely grown wine grapes in the northeastern United States. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to a few fruiting canes. Harvest the pinkish-gold clusters in late summer for white wine, sparkling base, or fresh juice.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in most well-drained soils.

directions: This hardy, productive American variety is easy to grow and tolerant of a range of soils. Train to a trellis and thin clusters, since heavy set can cause the tight bunches to split in wet weather. Harvest the pale green-gold berries in early fall for white wine and juice.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with room to sprawl.

directions: This old slipskin variety is vigorous and productive, thriving on an arbor or strong trellis. Prune hard each winter to manage its rampant growth and keep fruiting wood renewed. Harvest the blue-black, musky berries in fall for fresh eating, juice, and rustic wine.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring.

directions: This hardy Cornell blue grape bears long, tapering clusters of spicy-sweet fruit. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes each dormant season. Harvest in mid fall when the berries turn deep blue for fresh eating, juice, and jelly, as the fruit also stores well.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny location.

directions: This early-ripening blue grape produces sweet, richly flavored clusters and shows good disease resistance. Train to a trellis and prune to renewal canes to keep production steady. Harvest in late summer to early fall when the berries color fully for fresh dessert use and juice.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: An early white grape with a mild, sweet flavor, Ontario ripens ahead of most labrusca types. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to fruiting canes each winter. Harvest the pale greenish-white clusters in late summer for fresh eating and juice.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring.

directions: This heirloom white grape bears compact clusters of greenish-yellow berries with a sprightly, slightly tart flavor. Grow it on a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes each dormant season. Harvest in early fall for fresh eating, juice, and light white wine.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring.

directions: This hardy seedless red grape produces firm, crisp berries with a fruity flavor and few seed traces. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes, thinning clusters for larger berries. Harvest in late summer when the fruit turns deep red and sweet for fresh dessert use.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This early seedless white grape bears crisp, sweet golden berries in compact clusters. Train to a trellis and prune to fruiting canes, thinning shoots for good air movement. Harvest in late summer when the berries turn amber for fresh eating and raisins.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring.

directions: This seedless red table grape produces loose clusters of sweet, mild pink-red berries. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes, thinning clusters for size. Harvest in late summer to early fall when fully colored for fresh dessert use.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: This productive seedless white grape bears large, tight clusters of sweet golden berries. Train to a trellis and thin clusters generously, since the vine sets heavily. Harvest in early fall when the fruit turns amber-gold for fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring.

directions: This cold-tolerant seedless white grape bears large berries with a mild, fruity flavor that mellows as it ripens. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes. Harvest in early fall when the clusters turn pale gold and sweet for fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This hardy Cornell seedless red grape produces medium berries with a distinctive strawberry-like flavor. Train to a trellis and prune to renewal canes, thinning clusters for size. Harvest in late summer when the fruit turns bright red for fresh dessert use.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; tolerates cold sites.

directions: One of the hardiest seedless grapes, Somerset ripens early with small pink berries and a sweet, fruity flavor. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes each dormant season. Harvest in late summer when the berries blush pink for fresh eating in northern gardens.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This University of Arkansas seedless grape bears large blue-black berries with a mild muscat flavor. Train to a trellis and prune to renewal canes, thinning clusters for larger fruit. Harvest in late summer when the berries color fully for crisp fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring.

directions: This Arkansas seedless white grape produces attractive medium clusters of firm, sweet green-gold berries. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes for steady production. Harvest in late summer to early fall when the fruit turns golden for fresh dessert use.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This early, hardy seedless grape bears blue-black berries with a sweet labrusca-muscat flavor. Train to a trellis and prune to renewal canes, thinning clusters to improve size. Harvest in mid to late summer for fresh eating and juice.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring.

directions: This seedless red grape produces firm, crisp berries with a mild fruity flavor and good disease resistance. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes, thinning clusters for larger fruit. Harvest in late summer when the berries turn bright red for fresh dessert use.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: This seedless blue-black grape bears medium clusters of sweet, spicy berries and colors up attractively. Train to a trellis and prune to renewal canes, thinning shoots for good airflow. Harvest in late summer when the berries turn fully blue for fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the danger of hard frost passes.

directions: This patented seedless white grape is famous for its sweet, vanilla, candy-like flavor. Grow it on a trellis in full sun and prune to renewal canes each dormant season, thinning clusters for size. Harvest in late summer when the green-gold berries reach full sweetness for fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: This popular East Asian grape bears very large, deep-purple slipskin berries with a rich, sweet flavor. Train to a trellis or pergola in full sun and thin clusters and berries to develop the signature size. Harvest in early fall when the berries turn near-black and slip easily from the skin.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sunny location.

directions: This early seedless vinifera bears large, tight clusters of crisp, pale green berries and does well in hot regions. Train to a trellis and prune to spurs or short canes, thinning the dense clusters to prevent rot. Harvest in early to midsummer when the berries turn translucent and sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained site.

directions: This vigorous seedless vinifera bears large clusters of reddish-purple berries and is a favorite for raisins and fresh eating. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to canes, since it fruits best on longer wood. Harvest in late summer to early fall when the berries darken and sweeten.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sunny location.

directions: Also sold as King's Ruby, this late seedless vinifera bears large clusters of firm, sweet red berries that store well. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to canes, thinning clusters for size. Harvest in fall when the berries develop deep red color and high sugar.

planting.note: Plant in spring in the warm, humid Southeast.

directions: This self-fertile black muscadine sets a heavy crop without a pollinator and works well as a pollinizer for female varieties. Train a single trunk to a high-wire trellis and prune to short spurs each dormant season. Harvest the sweet black berries in early fall as they ripen and drop when fully colored.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny southern site.

directions: This self-fertile bronze muscadine bears large, sweet fruit and produces a reliable crop on its own. Train to a high-wire trellis and prune to short spurs in late winter to renew fruiting wood. Harvest the golden-bronze berries in late summer to early fall as they soften and sweeten.

planting.note: Plant in spring in the humid Southeast.

directions: This early, self-fertile bronze muscadine is productive and disease resistant, ripening ahead of many varieties. Train to a high-wire trellis and prune to spurs each dormant season. Harvest the pinkish-bronze berries in late summer when sweet for fresh eating and wine.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm southern garden.

directions: This self-fertile bronze muscadine is a dependable, long-established variety popular for wine and juice. Train to a high-wire trellis and prune to short spurs to renew the fruiting wood each winter. Harvest the sweet bronze berries in early fall as they ripen unevenly over several weeks.

planting.note: Plant in spring in the warm, humid Southeast.

directions: This self-fertile black muscadine bears large, sweet berries and shows good cold hardiness for the species. Train to a high-wire trellis and prune to spurs each dormant season. Harvest the large black fruit in early fall when fully colored and slightly soft.

planting.note: Plant in spring near a self-fertile pollinizer.

directions: This female-flowered black muscadine produces very large, sweet berries prized for fresh eating. Because it needs pollen from a self-fertile variety nearby, plant a pollinizer within about fifty feet. Train to a high-wire trellis, prune to spurs, and harvest the jumbo black berries in early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm southern site.

directions: This unusual muscadine-bunch grape hybrid has ornamental, deeply cut foliage and is often grown on arbors. It is self-fertile and productive, needing a strong support for its vigorous growth. Prune to spurs each dormant season and harvest the small black berries in early fall for fresh use and wine.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: Known botanically as Durif, this vinifera produces small, thick-skinned berries in tight clusters that make deeply colored, tannic red wine. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to spurs, keeping an open canopy to reduce bunch rot. Harvest in fall when the berries reach full ripeness and sugar.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: This traditional Burgundian white produces crisp, high-acid wines and is more productive than Chardonnay. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to spurs or short canes each dormant season. Harvest the pale green-gold clusters in early to mid fall for bright, zesty white wine.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm site with good air drainage.

directions: Also called Lemberger, this late-ripening Central European red yields spicy, dark-fruited wines with firm acidity. Train to a trellis in full sun and prune to spurs, keeping the canopy open for even ripening. Harvest the blue-black clusters in mid to late fall once sugars are high.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the parent species of Concord-type grapes.

directions: This vigorous native vine is the wild ancestor of many American slipskin grapes and climbs strongly by tendrils. Give it a sturdy arbor or fence and prune hard in winter to keep it in bounds. Harvest the musky, aromatic dark berries in fall for juice, jelly, and fresh eating, or grow it for wildlife and naturalizing.

planting.note: Plant in spring in the warm south-central states.

directions: This heat- and drought-tolerant native of Texas and the south-central states is extremely vigorous and climbs high into trees. Provide a very strong support and prune hard, since the vine grows rampantly. Harvest the tart, thick-skinned dark berries in summer for jelly, juice, and country wine, handling the acidic fresh fruit with care.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once the danger of hard frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting at the depth it grew in the pot in well-drained soil and full sun. This naturally compact tree responds well to summer pruning, which keeps the large purple-black fruit within easy reach. Harvest when figs droop on the stem and feel soft with a drop of nectar at the eye.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into a warm, sheltered position.

directions: Choose a sunny wall or open spot with fast-draining soil and firm the roots in gently. Water steadily through fruit swell, then ease off as figs begin to color to concentrate the sweet, dark-red flesh. Pick the small deep-purple fruit when the necks bend and the skin softens.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once soil temperatures are reliably mild.

directions: Set the cutting into loose, well-drained ground in full sun and mulch the root zone. The green skin masks ripe fruit from birds, so watch instead for softening and a pink blush at the eye. Its strawberry-pink interior is best eaten fresh straight from the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this vigorous French variety tolerates cool-summer regions well.

directions: Give this vigorous grower room and a full-sun site with free drainage. It ripens elongated purple figs even in shorter, cooler seasons, making it a dependable choice for northern gardens. Harvest late summer into autumn as the long-necked fruit softens and darkens.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a warm, sheltered corner for best fruit set.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil against a warm aspect and water evenly during the growing season. This early French variety carries deep purple-red fruit with rich, sweet flesh and resists splitting in damp weather. Pick when the figs hang down and yield to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost in a hot, sunny location.

directions: Position this connoisseur's fig in the hottest spot you can offer with sharply drained soil. It rewards summer heat with small, intensely sweet dark figs prized for their berry-like flavor. Harvest only fully soft fruit, as its richness develops right at peak ripeness.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm, well-drained ground.

directions: This Spanish landrace fig thrives in Mediterranean-style heat and full sun with lean soil. Keep it evenly watered while fruit fills, then reduce water as the dark-skinned figs ripen for the best sugars. Gather when the fruit softens fully and the neck droops.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny, sheltered site.

directions: A green-skinned selection from the Balearic Islands, it prospers in hot sun and free-draining soil. Water steadily through summer and thin crowded shoots so light reaches the developing fruit. Harvest the amber-fleshed figs when their skin yellows slightly and they hang heavy.

planting.note: Plant in spring; well suited to humid, warm-summer regions.

directions: This Louisiana heirloom handles Gulf Coast humidity better than many European types. Plant in full sun with good drainage and mulch to steady soil moisture through the season. Its brown-purple figs with sweet amber flesh ripen over a long late-summer window.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, sunny spot with good drainage.

directions: Bred in the LSU program, this variety carries yellow-green figs with light, sweet flesh and good disease resistance for the Deep South. Give it full sun and steady moisture, mulching to protect surface roots. Harvest when the fruit softens and the skin turns pale gold.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sheltered location.

directions: This Louisiana selection produces medium light-brown figs with a small closed eye that shrugs off souring in humid climates. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and keep the root zone mulched. Pick the sweet, honeyed fruit when it softens and bends at the neck.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into fertile, sunny ground.

directions: An early-ripening dark fig valued for reliable production even in marginal fig climates. Plant in full sun with free drainage and prune in late winter to shape the framework. Harvest the rich, berry-flavored purple-black figs as they soften on the branch.
